documents.title: 1.1_Banquets of Ancient Egyptians_S
documents.content:
Banquets of Ancient Egyptians
The River Nile enabled the ancient Egyptian civilisation as fertile lands provided rich natural resources. The rich in ancient Egypt enjoyed luxurious lifestyle, to them, banquets and celebrations were important parts of their lives. Ancient Egyptian paintings illustrating scenes of banquets allowed us to explore the entertainment side of their lives.
Fine Clothes
Ancient Egyptians held banquets at different occasions, including religious festivals, weddings, born of babies, and even funerals. The rich held banquets simply for enjoyment, while attending guests would dress up delicately. They would wear linen clothes so fine that they were transparent, wigs and make-up would also be worn, they would also put on luxurious accessories on chests and legs together with bracelets, earrings and head cones.

Daily clothes worn by noble women in ancient Egypt.
Pleasant Fragrance
If you were in an ancient Egyptian banquet, you would find a pleasant smell in the air. The odour came from flowers prepared for the guests and, possibly, the head cones worn by the guests. Scholars believe that the headdresses were made of wax, as the material melt away slowly with body heat, odour from the wax would give off a charming fragrance.
In the past, head cones could only be seen in ancient Egyptian murals, some therefore argued that they were only artistic symbols, just as the halo on the head of a saint in Christian artworks. In 2009, archaeologists [考古學家] found mummies with head cones in Amarna, Egypt, this proved the existence of these headdresses for the first time. Studies on them found that they were hollow and seemed to be made of beeswax. However, no melted wax was found on the mummies’ heads. Some scholars believe that they were replicas as the mummies were found in a tomb of workers.

Ancient Egyptian women with head cones at banquets.
Gourmet Feasts
Fine food and wine were indispensable [不可缺少] for a banquet. Harvests of ancient Egypt were plentiful, and banquets of rich people were filled with different kinds of food. Even though meat dishes were considered luxurious, the host would provide them generously to entertain the guests. In addition to beef, there were also dishes with ducks, geese, goats, fish, and pigs. They were flavoured with rosemary, cumin, garlic, celery, cinnamon, mustard, honey, figs, and fruits. Besides, vegetables such as chickpeas, lentils, lettuce, cucumbers and onions, as well as a wide variety of breads, cakes and biscuits with cheese and butter were all served on delicate plates. To complement the food, different kinds of alcohol, like wine and beer, were also provided. Attention was also paid to utensils used by the guests, they included exquisite dishes and lotus-shaped blue glaze cups.

Ancient Egyptian Music and Dance
Music and dance made ancient Egyptians banquets livelier. At the banquet, musicians will play a variety of instruments, including stringed instruments such as harp, lyre, lute, as well as oboe like instruments (usually played by women) and drums to spice up the party. To allow better enjoyment, musicians would invite guests to sing and clap along with the song, or hand out simple percussion instruments such as tambourines and cymbals to allow guests to play the music together. Dancers who wore jewellery would also dance along while acrobats would show the flexibility of their body by tossing and jumping.

Female musician playing musical instruments at the banquet.

Dancers spicing up the party by dancing.
Ancient Egyptian parties allowed us to understand the liveliness of the ancient people. Sadly, most remaining murals only reflect the lives of nobles and wealthy merchants. For the commoners in ancient Egyptian society, their lives were definitely much harder.

documents.filename: 1.1_Did Ancient Egyptians Worship Onions_S.docx
documents.title: 1.1_Did Ancient Egyptians Worship Onions_S
documents.content:
Did Ancient Egyptians Worship Onions?
Onions are one of the foods that are commonly found in our daily lives. However, they were given special status in ancient Egypt. Why did something ordinary like the onion become an object of worship to the ancient Egyptians?
The History of Onions
Archaeologists and botanists [植物學家] do not know when onions first appeared, though many scholars believe that onions originated in Central Asia. Before farming and writing were invented, our human ancestors had already begun to eat onions.
Onions may be one of the earliest grown crops in the world. Compared to other foods, onions are easier to grow as they can adapt to different kinds of soils and climates. They were also less perishable [不易變壞] which made them easy to transport. People could even dry and preserve onions for droughts [旱災] and famines [饑荒]. Onions were grown by civilisations such as the Fertile Crescent civilisation and the Indus Valley civilisation. The ancient Egyptians also grew onions as early as 3500 BC. Both nobles and commoners ate onions in ancient Egypt.

The growing of onions has a long history in Egypt.
Symbols of Onions
Onions can be found in wall paintings on pyramids and tombs built during the Old Kingdom and New Kingdom periods. Some of these onions are peeled; some are unripe. They appear on banquet tables, as part of funeral offerings, and even on the altars of gods or in the hands of priests. As shown in these paintings, onions were not simply food of the ancient Egyptians but had important meanings to them. Part of an onion grows underground while the other part grows skyward [向天空]. This symbolises the onion’s ability to cross between the realms of the living and the dead [跨越生死兩界]. Therefore, onions were closely related to the Egyptian gods who governed the underworld and the afterlife, and so people would offer onions at rituals.

An ancient Egyptian wall painting showing guests sharing onions at a banquet.
Onions and Mummies
The ancient Egyptians believed in the afterlife. Death was not the end, but one step towards eternal life [永生]. A dead person’s soul may return to their body. Therefore, it would be necessary to preserve dead bodies well so that people’s lives could continue. Mummies made by the ancient Egyptians reflect such a view of life and death.
Furthermore, by cutting an onion into half, one can see the concentric circles [同心圓] inside. The circular pattern made the ancient Egyptians think of eternal life, so they often used onions when making mummies. Archaeologists have found onions in different parts of mummies, such as the pelvic region (骨盆), the chest, the ears, the eyes, the legs and the feet. Onions were placed in the eye sockets of the mummy of Pharaoh Ramesses IV.

A cross-section of an onion shows a set of concentric circles which were regarded as a symbol of eternal life by the ancient Egyptians.
To restore the ability of the dead to breathe and speak in the afterlife, the ancient Egyptians would perform the ‘mouth-opening ritual’ [開口儀式] for them. Onions were used to purify (淨化) the mouth in the ritual. The Book of the Dead described how the ancient Egyptians put onions into the mouths and nostrils of the dead.

Anubis, the death god, leads the dead to the world of afterlife. The priest performs the ‘mouth-opening ritual’.
The Magical Power of Onions
According to Egyptian myths, Osiris, the afterlife god, gave birth to his son Horus. The milk teeth of Horus fell to the ground and became onion bulbs. Other tales claim that onions came from the ‘Eye of Horus’ – a symbol of health, prosperity and protection. In some rituals, the priests would use onions to represent the teeth and the eyes of Horus as they recited spells to defend against the sharp teeth and venom [毒液] of snakes.

An Eye of Horus amulet
Besides, the ancient Egyptians believed that onions could give strength. As a result, builders of pyramids were given onions as food. Herodotus, an ancient Greek historian, mentioned in his Histories that Egyptian writing on pyramid walls showed the amounts of radishes, onions and garlic given to the builders. Later scholars believe that since a large labour force was needed to build pyramids, the ancient Egyptians hoped that foods like onions could keep the workers healthy.

A wall painting showing how the ancient Egyptians transported a large statue
From a small onion, we can see the wisdom of the ancient Egyptians. Despite their lack of modern scientific knowledge, they were so observant and imaginative that they created a unique culture which has become a treasure of human civilisation.

documents.filename: 1.1_Discovering Stone Age Life through Cave Painting_S.docx
documents.title: 1.1_Discovering Stone Age Life through Cave Painting_S
documents.content:
Discovering Stone Age Life through Cave Painting
What do you think you can find if you follow your dog? On 12 September 1940, there were four boys who brought their dogs to a forest in France. Suddenly, the dog stopped in front of a tree and fell down a hole. The boys were so excited. They followed the dog and were surprised by the scenes in front of them. Several hundred large animals were painted on the cave walls, which were dramatically marvellous.

The Lascaux prehistoric cave paintings were discovered accidentally. Many of them depicted large animals that are now extinct.
The 15,000- to 17,000-year-old paintings were originally from the Paleolithic age. There are more than 1,500 engravings and 500 drawings having been documented. Of the drawings, over 100 of them are animals including aurochs, horses, bears, wolves, deer, birds and human. The only human figure is a man with a bird’s head who appears to have been felled by the bison. Abstract imagery like signs, symbols are also common. But some of them are yet to be interpreted.

Among the prehistoric caves, paintings and engravings are very common. Lascaux's cave paintings are well-known for its large-scale wall paintings. The photo shows a bull which is 5.2 metres long, the largest animal discovered so far in cave art.

The female Chinese horse with signs of pregnancy may have been an effort to encourage reproduction. More importantly, the primitive artists finished the paintings with the use of simple stone tools, natural pigments only under poor lighting conditions.
According to the researches about cave paintings, those threatening animals like tigers, leopards, jackals and wolves were painted on the exterior walls. However, peaceful animals like bulls, horses and deer were painted on the interior walls. Such a composition is regarded as the incorporation of religious rituals into the hunting traditions when the ancient humans would pray for victory before hunting. Also, the drawings also represent an understanding and a fear of nature.

The handprints in ancient cave art most often belonged to women due to the sizes of hands. Therefore, it is possible that the artists left them as records after paintings. During prehistoric times, women mostly stayed in the caves to take care of their families and prepare food. Therefore, they had much more time to do artistic works than the men.
The cave complex was opened to the public in 1948. Nevertheless, the large number of visitors had visibly damaged the paintings. Consequently, the cave was closed to the public in 1963. In order to satisfy people’s curiosity of the prehistoric lives, the French government had made copies of the wall paintings without harming the originals. But they were relatively small and simple. In 2016, the new International Center for Cave Art, Lascaux was opened. The new, complete and full-scale replica of Lascaux hopes to reproduce the exact state of the cave when it was found.
The Lascaux Cave is important to understand the human lives in the Old Stone Age. It turned out to be one of the world's best examples of prehistoric art with the use of very basic conditions and tools.

documents.filename: 1.1_Discovery at Egyptian pyramids_S.docx
documents.title: 1.1_Discovery at Egyptian pyramids_S
documents.content:
Discovery at Egyptian pyramids
Egyptian pyramids are one of the great achievements of the Egyptian civilisation. However, the mystery of how the Egyptians built the pyramids has not been solved. In 2016, the Egyptian Museum in Cairo put on display the country's oldest papyruses, which date back 4,500 years, detailing the daily life of the pyramid-builders. It allows people to know more about the Egyptian civilisation.
INCLUDEPICTURE "http://imgs.ntdtv.com/pic/2016/7-18/p7557871a832627671.jpg" \* MERGEFORMATINET

Egyptian pyramids
The papyri displayed were found in 2013 inside caves in the ancient Red Sea port by an Egyptian-French mission. They’re the oldest ever to be discovered, being written in the fourth dynasty of King Khufu. Fragment of papyrus written in hieratic and hieroglyphs.
INCLUDEPICTURE "http://www.coastweek.com/3929-papyri.jpg" \* MERGEFORMATINET

Papyrus had been used until about 8th century. It was replaced due to the spread of the movable type printing press. Papyrus was used this kind of paper by the 9th century when the cheap paper from Arab was imported.
INCLUDEPICTURE "http://media.buzzle.com/media/images-en/gallery/historical-events/450-178588006-egyptian-hieroglyph.jpg" \* MERGEFORMATINET INCLUDEPICTURE "http://www.crystalinks.com/hieratic.jpg" \* MERGEFORMATINET


Ancient Egyptian language was mainly written in four different scripts: hieroglyphs, hieratic and demotic. The left one was written in hieroglyphs while the right one was hieratic.
One of the papyruses belonged to a senior employee named Marr who played a role in the building of the pyramid and it covered a period of three months of his job, providing information about his duties - including transporting rocks through the River Nile and its canals.
More importantly, one of the document reveals the financial balances of ancient Egypt. Revenue was written in red, while what was paid to workers would be written in black. The other papyruses show the distribution of food portions for workers, including one showing in clear Egyptian hieroglyphs the number of sheep brought in.
INCLUDEPICTURE "http://i.dailymail.co.uk/i/pix/2016/07/15/17/36455C2F00000578-3692283-The_documents_also_list_revenues_transferred_from_various_Egypti-a-11_1468599822301.jpg" \* MERGEFORMATINET

The documents also list revenues transferred from various Egyptian provinces to feed pyramid builders and pay their wages.
One of the Egyptian experts pointed out that these show the administrative power and the central nature of the state at the time of Khufu. That’s why it could provide a great assistance to the building of the pyramid.
INCLUDEPICTURE "http://i-cias.com/e.o/slides/khufu01.jpg" \* MERGEFORMATINET

The Pharaoh Khufu
The Great Pyramid at Giza is the only one of the seven wonders that is still standing today. More than 100 pyramids have been found in Egypt. Egyptologists believe that the pyramid of Khufu with 146.5 metres high, was built during the fourth dynasty Egyptian Pharaoh Khufu as a tomb for a 20-year period.
Traditionally, the Egyptian pyramids were thought to be built by slaves. However, the newly discovered tomb in the few years ago provided supporting evidence that slaves did not build the ancient monuments. From 1990s, the mud-brick tombs were uncovered near the Giza pyramids, and they were different from the tombs of common people and slaves which were simply built using stones.
INCLUDEPICTURE "http://www.guardians.net/hawass/workmen's_tombs/images/worktomb1.jpg" \* MERGEFORMATINET INCLUDEPICTURE "http://www.guardians.net/hawass/workmen's_tombs/images/worktomb5.jpg" \* MERGEFORMATINET


The newly found tombs were built beside the king's pyramid, which indicates that these people were not slaves but the workers died during the construction of pyramids.
It was found that the workers regularly ate meat and worked three-month rotating shifts. They were so well regarded they were also given the honour of being buried in mud brick tombs within the shadow of the sacred pyramids they worked on if they died during construction. The site indicated that the 10,000-strong army of workers ate 21 cattle and 23 sheep a day provided by the farms so that they could obtain tax reduction. There is also evidence to suggest that the labourers not only called themselves “friends of Khufu” but they were also paid for their work. Therefore, it is believed that the long-standing mystery of how Egypt's pyramids were built will be solved with new discoveries.

documents.filename: 1.1_The City that Offended God - The Old City of Babylon_S.docx
documents.title: 1.1_The City that Offended God - The Old City of Babylon_S
documents.content:
The City that Offended God - The Old City of Babylon
In 2019, the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO, 聯合國教科文組織) included the old city of Babylon, which was located in present-day Iraq, in the World Heritage List. This old city has a long history. It witnessed the ups and downs of the Fertile Crescent civilisation. According to the Bible, it was a city that had even offended God.

The ruins of the old city of Babylon in Iraq
From a Small Town to the Centre of an Empire
The Fertile Crescent civilisation appeared about 6,000 years ago. It was one of the oldest civilisations in the world. It existed between the River Euphrates and the River Tigris. The land between the two rivers is called Mesopotamia. Different peoples built city-states and kingdoms upon this fertile piece of crescent-shaped land. And the history of Babylon dates back to the Akkadian Empire in the 24th century BC. At that time, Babylon was only a small town in the empire.
Until the establishment of the Old Babylonian Empire, the city of Babylon became the capital of it. During the period of the Assyrian Empire, the city of Babylon was destroyed by warfare, and it was rebuilt afterwards. During the period of the New Babylonian Empire, King Nebuchadnezzar II further expanded the city. There were many magnificent [雄偉] buildings in the city. It became a famous old city in history.

King Nebuchadnezzar II mandated (下令) the construction of the Ishtar Gate. This picture shows a replica of it, which is stored in the Pergamon Museum, Berlin.
The Hanging Gardens in the Old City of Babylon
The Hanging Gardens were the most well-known architecture in the city of Babylon. The Hanging Gardens were regarded as one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. But archaeologists [考古學家] are still unable to find out its exact location. Legend has it that the Hanging Gardens were built during the rule of Nebuchadnezzar II. It was built with the same scenery of the hometown of his queen, Amytis [阿米蒂斯], to please her. According to the ancient sources, the Hanging Gardens were near the palace, they were built upon platforms of different heights, this made the gardens appear to suspend in the air [懸在半空]. They also had special irrigation system. Therefore, the Hanging Gardens demonstrated the advanced craftsmanship and technology at that time.

An imaginative painting of the Hanging Gardens
A Ziggurat for Worshipping the Patron God (守護神)
There were religious buildings in the city of Babylon. People built ziggurats to worship deities. Etemenanki [埃特曼安吉塔廟] was used to worship the patron god of Babylon, Marduk [馬杜克]. This ziggurat was once destroyed during the Assyrian conquest of Babylon, and it was rebuilt after the New Babylonian Empire had established. 'Etemenanki' means ‘the house of the foundation of heaven and earth’ in Sumerian. It was a very grand building. Its bottom was a square that had side lengths of 91 metres. There were seven levels in total. Its height was 91 metres, and it was twice the height of other ziggurats. There were flights of stairs surrounding the ziggurat, which allowed people to climb up to the top level to the temple of Marduk.

The patron god of Babylon, Marduk
Why Was it Named 'The City that Offended God'?
Some scholars consider Etemenanki to be the prototype [原型] of the Tower of Babel [巴別塔] in the Bible. According to the Genesis, the ancestors of human spoke a single language. They settled in the fertile plain in Shinar (Babylon). In the hope of making a name for themselves and preventing people from scattering, they decided to construct a city and a tower that could reach the heavens. They used brick instead of stone, and tar [石漆] for mortar [尼灰], to build the tower. When God came down to see the city and the tower, God was discontent with the arrogance of human. God thought that if people spoke a single language, they would be able to build the tower, and then nothing they planned to do would be impossible for them. Therefore, God confused the languages of the people, which made them not able to communicate. God also scattered them all over the world, so they stopped building the city. The word 'Babel' means 'confusion' [混亂] in Hebrew.

The Tower of Babel depicted in a Renaissance painting
The incident of the Tower of Babel showed that human disobeyed God's will. They hoped to make a name for themselves on their own. This offended God and they were punished. Finally, the tower of Babel was abandoned and then collapsed. Etemenanki also could not stand the ravages of time. In 331 BC, after Alexander the Great conquered Babylon, he ordered to rebuild the ziggurat. However, the reconstruction project ended in smoke after his death, and the city of Babylon gradually declined in turmoil. To this day, people can only imagine the prosperity enjoyed by this city through the ruins.

documents.filename: 1.1_The Daily Lives of Mesopotamian Priests and Priestesses_S.docx
documents.title: 1.1_The Daily Lives of Mesopotamian Priests and Priestesses_S
documents.content:
The Daily Lives of Mesopotamian Priests and Priestesses
Mesopotamia was one of the earliest centres of civilisation in human history. In the past, people believed that the gods made humans out of clay, and that humans existed solely to serve the gods. Thus, the priests of Mesopotamia became key figures of that time.
Unique characters in ancient society
Communicating with the guardian god [守護神] of a city was the main duty of a priest. Only priests knew the rituals for interacting with gods so they were seen as their representatives. The gods gave Mesopotamian kings the right to rule a country; as such, kings needed to be granted power from a priest through getting a sceptre from him [權杖], in order to legitimately [名正言順] be crowned. It is clear that the importance of priests in ancient society was only second to the king; priests had greater powers than other members of the ruling class (e.g. politicians and soldiers).

The statue of King Gudea is inscribed with a dedication [獻詞] to the guardian god. This shows that political power in Mesopotamia cannot be separated from religion.
Duties of priests
Priests had different responsibilities depending on their ranks. High-ranking priests held rituals [祭祀儀式], used divination [占卜] and astrology [觀察天象] to predict the future and understand the will of the gods. They also oversaw the work of lower-ranking priests. Other priests led the people in singing holy songs, performed exorcisms [驅魔儀式], built statues for the ziggurat, performed cleaning duties, etc.

A water basin used for purification rituals [淨化儀式]. The fish-shaped people on the surface are believed to be priests wearing costumes.
Aside from religious work, priests also contributed greatly to the running of a city-state. As the managers of the ziggurat, they directly controlled a large portion of the country’s wealth. Part of the land of city-states belonged to the ziggurat, and thus priests could use the resources on that land to conduct commercial activities, increasing the wealth of the ziggurat. Moreover, there were tributes [貢品] from citizens to the gods in the ziggurat, and the spoils of war [戰利品] would also be sent to the ziggurat first. The priests controlling the ziggurat were among the powerful people in Mesopotamian society. Priests also treated the sick in the outer courts of the ziggurat, and were both teachers and managers of schools in the ziggurat.

The ziggurat, which was the site of ritual sacrifices, was the central building of a city-state. The above picture shows the Great Ziggurat of Ur.

Statues found in the ruins of a temple. They are likely offerings to the guardian deity.
The gender of Mesopotamian priests
Priesthood was hereditary [世襲的] so those who became priests usually belonged to noble families. Although women had a low status in ancient societies, they could become priestesses. According to Hammurabi’s Laws, priestesses could inherit [繼承] property from their fathers. The most famous priestess was undoubtedly Enheduanna, daughter of Sargon of Akkad. As the high priestess of Ur, she composed a series of poems known as the Sumerian Temple Hymns.

Stone disc of Enheduanna. She is the central figure, and is watching a ceremony along with three attendants.

documents.filename: 1.1_Trivia Hammurabi’s Laws_S.docx
documents.title: 1.1_Trivia Hammurabi’s Laws_S
documents.content:
Trivia: Hammurabi’s Laws

Have you ever heard a penalty of “an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth”? In 2008, an Iranian man had been sentence to be blinded for acid attack after he blinded a woman. It was the first time that the court applied Iran’s sharia eye-for-an-eye laws. In 2015, the first punishment was carried out and thereafter, it has become one of the strictest laws in Iran.
In fact, the law could be traced back to Old Babylonian Empire 3,700 years ago. The king Hammurabi, found it difficult to deal with vast number of cases of appeal himself. Therefore, he ordered to collect previous law codes and conventions, and enacted Hammurabi’s Laws. He carved the laws on stone pillars and sent the pillars to all cities within the empire to demonstrate justice. The code contains 282 laws in cuneiform.
Many laws are based on the principle of “an eye for an eye, a tooth for a tooth”. For example, if a person broke someone's leg, that person's leg was broken as punishment. If the poorly constructed house causes the death of the homeowner's son, the carpenter's son shall be executed. Thus, Hammurabi’s Laws is really very harsh. In the following, let’s understand more about the society through this code of laws.

Hammurabi’s pillar of laws. In about 1,760 BC. It is on display at the Louvre, a museum in Paris, France.
Old Babylonian Religion and Superstition
From the above pillar, Hammurabi is standing in prayer before the Sun God. The Sun God is giving the symbols of justice and righteousness, a staff and a ring to Hammurabi. Therefore, it is regarded as a delegation of political authority to the king. . In the preface to the law, the power of Hammurabi is also restated. These represent that the king had already made use of the divine right of kings [君權神授] to proof his legitimacy.
Besides, god judge was also very common in handling the cases. If the defendant is not drowned after being thrown into the river, he is not found guilty as the river has cleaned him already. However, the informer will be put to death. It reflects that the old Babylonians are really superstitious.

The Cuneiform script on the pillar.
Contradictions in Demonstrating Social Justice
Actually, there are three classes referred to in the code: (1) men or persons, who are property owners and the wealthy and upper classes; (2) freemen, who are poor men, serfs, or retainers, but able to own property and slaves; and (3) slaves. The code aims to protect the interests of the first class and therefore, the laws varied according to social class and gender. If the victims are from the lower classes, then the lawbreakers can simply pay a fine for punishment. If a man kill a pregnant slave, he will be fined only. But if the woman is a freeman, the daughter of the man will be executed instead.
However, the king desired to use the law code to achieve social equality. Apart from using strict laws to reorganise the society, he set up rules like minimum wage for workers including farmers, doctors and poor slaves. Life-long slavery was also abolished. They enjoyed freedom and the right to be protected.
An Influential Law Code: Hammurabi’s Laws
After the fall of Babylon, Hammurabi left a great legacy. His system of laws are seen throughout history and today. For example, presumption of innocence [無罪推斷] and trial by jury [陪審團] are important principles. Besides, the concept of revenge as discussed above is still strong in Muslim countries nowadays. In conclusion, the law code is considered to be one of the most valuable finds of human existence.

documents.filename: 1.1_《漢模拉比法典》的冷知識_S.docx
documents.title: 1.1_《漢模拉比法典》的冷知識_S
documents.content:
《漢模拉比法典》的冷知識

究竟世界上是否有「以牙還牙、以眼還眼」的刑罰？2008年，伊朗一名婦女在被潑酸導致眼盲後，法庭首次宣告對加害人採取伊斯蘭律法的「以眼還眼」制裁，即被判刑的男子必須以強酸弄瞎自己的雙眼。伊朗更在2015年首次執行此刑法，自此成為該國一項嚴厲的懲罰。
事實上，此刑法可追朔至3,700多年前的古巴比倫王國。當時的漢模拉比王由於感到自己難以處理數量龐大的申訴案件，因此他下令收集過往的法律條文及社會約定俗成的習慣，制訂了《漢模拉比法典》，刻在神殿的石柱上，體現公義。法典共有282條條文，用楔形文字寫成。
不少條文以「以牙還牙、以眼還眼」作為處理原則。例如，被人打斷了腿，也要把對方的腿打斷。如果房屋倒塌，壓死了房主的兒子，建造這所房屋的人得拿自己的兒子抵命。因此，我們可以得知當時法律的嚴厲。然而，這只是單單一部嚴明法典嗎？以下讓我們透過《漢模拉比法典》的小知識來了解巴比倫時期的社會特色吧！

漢模拉比法典碑，約公元前1760年。現存法國羅浮宮內。
古巴比倫的宗教與迷信
從石碑可見上方漢模拉比王以祈禱的姿勢站著，太陽神正將象徵司法和王權的測桿和指環遞給漢模拉比王，因此被理解為太陽神授於王。法典的序言亦重申漢模拉比是上天授與權力的王，足見國王已懂利用來宣傳其統治合法性。
另外根據用來處理巫盅之罪的水審法，犯案的被告人被投入河中，如果他沒有被溺死，就意味著河水已為他「洗白」，即為無罪，告發者應處死刑。由此，當時以神判的方式來處理案件的情況是很普遍的，反映了古巴比倫人非常迷信。

石碑上鐫刻的楔形文字條文。
彰顯公義之間的矛盾
漢模拉比法典的建立實際上是為了維護奴隸主階級的統治地位，保護統治階級的利益。當時，不同等級的人，在法律上的地位有高低之分。巴比倫人被分貴族、自由民及奴隸三等。如果受害人的階層較低，那犯法者只需付罰款便可了事。另外，條文更反映了男女不平等的問題。如果一個男人殺死懷孕的婢女，他會被處以罰金；但如果這位孕婦是自由人，犯罪者的女兒將被處死以為補償，而非本人。這是奴隸制及男性主導社會下的必然結果。
但是，漢模拉比國王希望透過法典來令社會達到公平公義。除了以嚴苛的法律來整頓社會外，他更訂立了一些規條，例如不同職業有都有最低的薪酬，終身奴役被廢除等。因此，農民、醫生，甚至低下的奴隸都能獲得最低工資，奴隸更有自由及受保護的權利。
影響深遠的《漢模拉比法典》
由美索不達米亞時期直到現在，我們都可以看到《漢模拉比法典》的影子。許多現代法律原則如「無罪推斷」、公民陪審團等都是由此起源。此外，在古代，正義與懲罰以「同態報復」為重心。直到現在，回教世界不少國家至今仍以同樣的方法來處理刑罰，也因而受到不少人道主義者的批評。總而言之，作為世上最早的一部比較具有系統的法典，其價值是不可言喻的。

documents.filename: 1.1_冒犯上帝的城巿——古巴比倫_S.docx
documents.title: 1.1_冒犯上帝的城巿——古巴比倫_S
documents.content:
冒犯上帝的城巿——古巴比倫
2019年，聯合國教科文組織將位於現今伊拉克的古城巴比倫列入世界遺產名錄。這座古城歷史悠久，不但見證了新月沃土文明的興衰，根據《聖經》記載，這更是一座曾冒犯上帝的城巿。

位於伊拉克的古巴比倫城遺址
從小鎮到王國中心
新月沃土文明出現於六千年前，是世上最古老的文明之一，由幼發拉底河和底格里斯河孕育而成，兩河流經的地方稱為「米索不達米亞」。在這片新月形狀的肥沃土地上，不同民族先後建立了城邦和王國，而巴比倫的歷史可追溯至公元前24世紀建立的阿卡底亞帝國，當時的巴比倫只是帝國治下的一個小鎮。
直到古巴比倫王國建立後，巴比倫城發展成為王國的中心。在亞述帝國時期，巴比倫城一度受戰火破壞，後來獲重建。在新巴比倫王國時前，國王尼布甲尼撒二世更進一步擴建巴比倫城，城內雄偉建築林立，成為歷史上著名的古城。

國王尼布甲尼撒二世下令修建伊斯塔城門，圖為柏林帕加馬博物館內的重建品。
古巴比倫城的空中花園
提及巴比倫城的建築，最著名的莫過於「空中花園」。空中花園被譽為古代世界七大奇蹟之一，但至今考古學家尚未找出其確實位置。相傳空中花園由尼布甲尼撒二世下令建造，並按照王后阿米蒂斯的故鄉景物來設計，以解她的思鄉之苦。據一些古代文獻記載，空中花園位於王宮附近，修築在不同高度的平台上，使花園看起來恍如懸在半空之中，並設有獨特的灌溉系統，體現了當時的高超工藝和科技水平。

一幅描繪空中花園的意想畫作
供奉守護神的塔廟
巴比倫城內亦建有宗教建築，人們興建塔廟來供奉神祇，埃特曼安吉塔廟（Etemenanki）就是用來供奉巴比倫的守護神馬杜克（Marduk）。這座塔廟曾因亞述人攻打巴比倫城而損毀，在新巴比倫王國建立後重建。「埃特曼安吉」在蘇美爾語中指「天地基台之所」，是一座非常宏大的建築，底部呈正方形，每邊長九十一米，共有七層，高九十一米，其高度大約是其他塔廟的兩倍。外層建有階梯，可以繞塔而上，至頂層高台上的馬杜克神廟。

巴比倫的守護神馬杜克
為何會被稱為「冒犯上帝的城巿」？
一些學者認為埃特曼安吉塔廟就是《聖經》中提及的「巴別塔」的原型。據《創世紀》的記載，人類的祖先本來只說同一種語言，他們在示拿（即巴比倫）的肥沃平原上定居，決定修建一座城和一座通天的高塔，以彰顯他們的名聲，並避免眾人分散。他們以磚為石頭，以石漆當尼灰，開始建城築塔。上帝降臨人間，看見世人所建的城和塔，不滿人類的狂妄自大。祂認為人類說同一種語言，就能築起巨塔，日後他們想做其他事，也會無所不能。於是，上帝讓他們的語言混亂起來，言語不通，人們也分散到各地去，不再建築這座城。而「巴別」在希伯來語中，意思是「混亂」。

一幅描繪巴別塔的文藝復興時期畫作
巴別塔事件反映了世人違背上帝的旨意，希望依靠人的能力立下自己的名望，因此冒犯了上帝，並受到懲罰。巴別塔最終停工倒塌，而歷史上的埃特曼安吉塔廟也逃不過歲月的摧殘。公元前331年，亞歷山大大帝攻佔巴比倫後，曾下令重建塔廟，可惜在他死後，重建工程無疾而終，巴比倫城在動蕩不休之中亦逐漸衰落。時至今日，人們只能透過遺址上留存下來的土磚建築物，想像這座古城曾經有過的繁華輝煌。

documents.filename: 1.1_古埃及人崇拜洋葱？_S.docx
documents.title: 1.1_古埃及人崇拜洋葱？_S
documents.content:
古埃及人崇拜洋葱？
洋葱是我們日常生活中常見的食物，但在古埃及，洋葱不僅是餐桌上的食物，更被賦予特別的地位。一顆平平無奇的洋葱，為何會成為古埃及人崇拜的對象？
洋葱的歷史
洋蔥究竟是在何時出現，考古學家和植物學家都沒有確切的答案，但不少學者均相信洋葱原產自中亞，而且在耕種和文字發明之前，人類的先祖已經開始進食洋葱。
洋葱可能是世上最早的農作物之一，因為相比其他食物，洋葱較易栽種，能夠適應不同的土壤和氣候，而且不易變壞，方便運送；人們更可將洋葱曬乾貯存，以應付日後的旱災和饑荒。在兩河流域、印度等古文明，人們均以洋葱為作物，而古埃及人種植洋葱則可以追溯至公元前3500年。不論是貴族或平民，古埃及人都經常食用洋葱。

洋葱的種植在埃及有悠久的歷史。
洋葱的象徵
在古王國時期和新王國時期建成的金字塔和陵墓中，從壁畫上都可見到洋蔥的蹤影：它們有的是去掉了皮的大洋葱，有的則尚未成熟；它們被放置在宴會桌上，也會作為喪禮的供物，甚至是展示在神祗的祭壇上，或祭司手中拿着洋葱。從這些壁畫可見，對於古埃及人來說，洋葱不僅是食物，而且具有重要的意義。洋葱的一部分長於泥土之下，一部分向天空生長，象徵了跨越生死兩界的能力，因此洋葱與古埃及掌管冥界和來生的神祗息息相關，人們會在祭典上獻上洋葱。

一幅古埃及壁畫，描繪宴會上賓客分享洋葱。
洋葱與木乃伊
古埃及人相信有死後的世界，死亡並非終結，而是通往永生的其中一站。當人死後，靈魂會回到肉身，因此屍體要好好保存，才能讓人的生命延續下去。古埃及人製作木乃伊，就呈現了這種生死觀。
而切開洋蔥，便能看見裡面同心圓的結構，這讓古埃及人聯想到永生，因此他們在製作木乃伊時，往往會使用洋葱。考古學家曾在木乃伊的骨盆、胸膛、耳朵、眼睛、腿部、腳底等位置發現洋葱。在法老王拉美西斯四世的木乃伊，洋葱就被放置在其眼窩處。

洋葱的橫切面呈現同心圓，古埃及人視為永生的象徵。
為了讓死者在死後恢復呼吸和說話的能力，古埃及人會為死者進行「開口儀式」，洋葱也在這儀式中擔任淨化口腔的角色，《死亡之書》就記載了古埃及人如何將洋葱放在死者口腔和鼻孔中。

地獄之神阿努比斯負責引導死者前往陰間，祭司正進行「開口儀式」。
洋葱的神奇力量
在埃及神話中，冥府之神奧西里斯生了兒子荷魯斯，他的乳齒落在地上，長出了洋葱球；在其他版本的描述中，洋葱的起源則來自「荷魯斯之眼」——具有健康、繁榮和保護的象徵。因此，在一些儀式中，祭司會以洋葱代表荷魯斯的牙齒和眼睛，念出咒語，以對抗毒蛇的尖牙和消除其毒液。

荷魯斯之眼護身符
此外，古埃及人認為洋葱具有促進力量生長的能力，因此建築金字塔的工人獲派洋葱作為糧食。古希臘歷史學家希羅多德在其著作《歷史》中記載，金字塔上刻有古埃及文字，記錄了工人得到的蘿蔔、洋蔥和大蒜的數量；後世學者認為建築金字塔需要大量勞動力，而當時古埃及人就是希望透過洋葱等食物，以維持工人的健康。

一幅描繪古埃及人如何運送龐大雕像的壁畫
從一顆小小的洋葱，我們可以看見古埃及人的智慧，雖然他們欠缺現代的科學知識，但他們對事物的入微觀察和聯想，形成其獨特的文化，成為了人類文明的寶藏。

documents.filename: 1.1_古埃及人的派對_S.docx
documents.title: 1.1_古埃及人的派對_S
documents.content:
古埃及人的派對
尼羅河孕育了古埃及文明，肥沃的土地帶來了豐富的資源。埃及的富有階層能享受逸樂的生活，對他們來說，派對和慶典更是生活中重要的一部分。一些古埃及的壁畫描繪了派對的場面，讓我們能窺探他們享樂的一面。
華衣美服
古埃及人在不同的場合都會舉辦派對，例如宗教節日、婚禮、嬰兒出生，甚至是喪禮。富有階層更會純粹為了娛樂而舉辦派對，出席派對的賓客都會悉心裝扮，他們穿上精緻得透明的亞麻衣服，頭戴假髮，臉上化了妝，並戴上華麗的飾物，包括胸飾、臂飾、手鐲、耳環，還有特別的圓錐頭飾。

古埃及貴族婦女日常所穿的服飾
香氣襲人
如果你身處古埃及的派對，會發現空氣中瀰漫着芬芳的氣味。香氣除了來自主人家為來賓準備的鮮花外，也可能來自賓客所配戴的圓錐頭飾。有學者認為，這些頭飾可能是用蠟所造的，配戴者身上的熱力會慢慢把頭飾的蠟融化，並滲透至頭髮裡，散發出迷人香氣。
以往人們只能在古埃及壁畫中看見這種圓錐頭飾，因此有論述指它們只是一種藝術象徵，就像基督教作品中聖人頭上的光環一樣。2009年，有考古團隊在埃及阿瑪納（Amarna）發掘到頭戴圓錐頭飾的木乃伊，首次證實了它們真實存在。研究發現圓錐頭飾中空，似乎是用蜂蠟所造，不過並沒有在木乃伊頭上發現融掉了蠟的痕跡。由於木乃伊是在工人墓中被發現，因此也有學者認為那只是仿製品，而非實際上流社會人士所戴的圓錐頭飾。

古埃及婦女在宴會上會配戴圓錐頭飾
美食饗宴
派對上不可缺少的是美酒佳餚。古埃及的農業收成豐富，富有人家的派對上充滿各式各樣的美食。即使肉類在古埃及是一種奢侈的食物，但主人家仍會亳不吝嗇烹調肉類來款待賓客。除了牛肉外，還有鴨、鵝、山羊、魚和豬，並利用迷迭香、小茴香、大蒜、西芹、肉桂和芥末等來調味，再加上蜂蜜、無花果和水果，非常豐盛。此外，鷹嘴豆、扁豆、生菜、青瓜和洋葱等蔬菜，種類繁多的麵包、蛋糕和餅乾，還有芝士、牛油等，都一一盛載在精緻的碟子上。美食之外亦有美酒相配，宴會上提供大量的葡萄酒和啤酒。賓客所使用的器皿也非常講究，精緻華麗的碗碟，還有呈蓮花狀的藍色彩釉杯子。

古埃及藍色彩釉杯子

一幅古埃及壁畫，描繪宴會上一名女賓客給予另一名賓客食物。
輕歌曼舞
音樂和舞蹈讓古埃及人的派對顯得更熱鬧。在派對上，樂師會演奏各種樂器，包括豎琴、里拉琴、魯特琴等弦樂器，還有類似雙簧管的樂器（一般由女性吹奏），再加上鼓聲來炒熱派對的氣氛。樂師會邀請賓客一起唱歌、拍手，或利用鈴鼓和鈸這些簡單的敲擊樂器一同和唱，讓他們能樂在其中。配戴珠寶的舞者也會隨着悠揚的音樂翩翩起舞，更有雜技員展示靈活的翻騰跳躍動作，娛樂賓客。

女樂師在宴會上演奏樂器

舞蹈員在宴會上跳舞助慶
從古埃及人的派對中，我們可以看見遠古文明中生活多姿多彩的一面。不過，留存下來的壁畫大多只反映貴族和富商的生活，至於平民百姓的日子，自然是刻苦得多。

documents.filename: 1.1_古埃及金字塔的新發現_S.docx
documents.title: 1.1_古埃及金字塔的新發現_S
documents.content:
古埃及金字塔的新發現
金字塔是古埃及文明的最重要象徵之一。但是，金字塔的建造方式、過程，仍然留下了許多謎團。開羅博物館在2016年首次展出一批4,500年前埃及人建造金字塔的建築日誌及飲食記錄，讓大眾對古埃及文明有更深的認識。

埃及金字塔
展覽展出了已知最古老的紙草文獻，是由埃及和法國考古團隊於2013年在紅海邊發現的，屬埃及古王國時期庫夫法魯王時期的作品，由神碑體和僧侶體寫成。

紙草一直使用到大約8世紀，後來由於造紙術的傳播而逐漸被取代。在埃及，莎草紙一直使用到9世紀，直至被從阿拉伯的廉價紙張傳入。


古埃及文字分為神碑體、僧侶體和世俗體。左為神碑體，右為僧侶體。
紙草上記錄的是法魯庫夫王年代的公文。其中最珍貴的一份是來自一位負責監督建造金字塔的官員。他的日誌描述了在他工作的3個月期間，工人每天的搬運情況。首先，他們從尼羅河東岸礦石場採集石灰岩塊上船，日復一日的穿過尼羅河、運河等運送到開羅修建金字塔的工地，建造吉薩大金字塔。

文件還有各地區收入的會計記錄，以及向工人支付的薪酬帳目。
更重要的是，其中一份文件可以發現古埃及財務記錄的狀況及規範。例如：收入用紅字書寫；而發給工人的薪酬則用黑字寫下。另外一份文件則記錄了法魯王向修建金字塔的工人每日供給食品等物資的數量。另外，上面亦詳細及清晰地記錄了購買羊隻的數目。
一位埃及博物館專家指出，這批文獻反映出古王國時期埃及已具備高效的行政管理系統，能夠為給予如修建金字塔一樣龐大工程提供緊密的後勤保障。

法魯王基俄普斯（又稱庫夫王）
埃及金字塔被譽為「古代世界七大奇觀」之一，亦是唯一仍然存在的建築。埃及境內到目前為止總共發現了一百座以上的金字塔，而最大的一座是便是第四王朝法魯王庫夫修建的吉薩金字塔。這座金字塔原高146.5米，用了約20年的時間才建成。
傳統的觀點都認為埃及金字塔是由大量奴隸建造。但近年考古有新的發現，金字塔並非由奴隸所造。1990年代開始，考古學家在吉薩金字塔群附近，陸續發現興建金字塔的工人墓穴。它們用泥和磚搭建，呈現錐形、表面覆蓋白色灰泥，有別於古時用石頭興建的平民和奴隸墓地。


胡夫金字塔位於金字塔旁的新墓群，埋葬的是興建金字塔期間死亡的工人，推翻了金字塔是由奴隸建造的說法。
經研究發現，這些工人每天有肉吃，每3個月輪班一次，加上死後可埋在法老王的金字塔旁，足見他們非但不是奴隸，而且有良好的生活待遇。從墓群數量上，考古學家估計，當時約有1萬名工人負責建塔。由於體力消耗大，當地農場每天提供他們21頭牛和23頭羊當食物，以便減稅。另外，這些墓穴的牆壁上更發現了一些工人的塗鴉，他們稱自己是「法魯王庫夫的朋友」，證明了工人為自由人的身份。相信隨著更多古埃及文物的發掘和出土，金字塔的謎團將將會一一被解開。

documents.filename: 1.1_從洞窟壁畫看石器時代人類的生活_S.docx
documents.title: 1.1_從洞窟壁畫看石器時代人類的生活_S
documents.content:
從洞窟壁畫看石器時代人類的生活
跟着小狗的腳步，你猜想可以發現甚麼？1940年9月12日，法國多爾多涅省蒙特涅克村的4個男孩帶着他們的小狗到森林遊玩。突然，小狗停在一株倒下的樹幹旁不斷挖，一不小心便掉進洞內。其中一個小孩呼叫：「下面會不會有寶藏？」於是，他們開始興奮起來。滑進洞口後，眼前的景像完全超乎了他們的想像。一個洞穴竟然有如此龐大的壁畫，各種各樣的動物圖畫不斷向前延伸。

拉斯科洞窟壁畫意外被發現，大部分描繪現代已滅絕的大型動物。
拉斯科洞窟壁畫約完成於15,000年至17,000年前，出自舊石器時代晚期。洞內總共有近1,500個岩石雕刻和500餘幅繪畫。這些繪畫中有100多幅是動物繪畫，內容包括牛、馬、熊、狼、鹿、鳥與人。唯一的人物形象是一個被野牛撞翻在地的人。另外還有一些抽象符號，至今仍然無法考究和解釋其根源。

在眾多的史前洞穴中，壁畫一般是採用繪畫和雕刻的方式。拉斯科洞壁畫的特別之處是規模宏大，圖為公牛廳裡的大公牛。最大的長達5米，是世界上壁畫之最。

拉斯科洞窟壁畫的馬中，最令人囑目的是因形體頗似中國的蒙古馬種而得名的「中國馬」。畫中的馬正處於懷孕期，這與祈求增加繁殖的觀念有關。更重要的是，畫作都是原始藝術家只能使用原始工具如石器及礦物質顏料、以及在簡陋的照明條件下完成。
根據對洞穴繪畫佈局的研究，凡是對人類有威脅的動物，如虎、豹、豺、狼等，大都繪製在朝外的岩壁上，而溫順的牛、馬、鹿等，大都繪製在朝裡的岩壁上。到目前為止，如此的佈局被認為是帶有宗教意味的。原始人類在狩獵前會透過儀式來祈求勝利，希望能夠活着帶回獵物。這些畫亦表達了當時的人們對自然力量的恐懼感和理解。

有人認為，大部分壁畫是出於女性之手。因為手印的大小和手指的粗細都與女性的手掌非常相似。因此，手印可能是畫家在完成作品後留下的痕跡。在遠古時代，女性多留在洞穴照顧家庭及準備食物，相比男性有較多時間進行藝術創作。
洞窟在1948年對外開放，但由於參觀人數過多，空氣等自然條件受到破壞，損害史前人類壁畫。因此法國在1963年開始對外關閉洞窟。但是，為了滿足人們對史前生活的好奇心，過去法國政府也曾複製一些岩洞繪畫供民眾參觀，但規模較小，細節也較模糊。2016年，新的拉斯科洞窟壁畫復刻展覽館揭幕。參與的藝術家仿效石器時代人類，徒手或以簡單工具繪畫，只有小部分用畫筆繪畫，希望能重現壁畫原本的面貌。
拉斯科洞窟被譽為史前藝術文明的搖籃，反映了舊石器時代人類的生活。當時人們已經懂得利用岩壁天然的起伏以及簡單的工具來繪製狩獵的情景及畫像，將洞穴藝術發揮到極致。

documents.filename: 1.1_米索不達米亞祭司的日常生活_S.docx
documents.title: 1.1_米索不達米亞祭司的日常生活_S
documents.content:
米索不達米亞祭司的日常生活
米索不達米亞是人類歷史上最早期的文明中心之一。古人認為神使用粘土製造了人類，而人類存在世上的意義就是為了侍奉神明。因此，米索不達米亞的神職人員是那個時代的關鍵人物。
古代社會的特殊角色
與城邦的守護神溝通並傳達其意願，是祭司最主要的職責。只有祭司懂得與神明交往的儀式，所以他們被視為「神的代表人」。米索不達米亞國王管理城邦的權利由神授予，因此國王需要經過授權儀式從祭司獲得權杖，才能夠名正言順地登上王位。單從這一點，古代神權社會的祭司重要性僅次於國王，比其他統治階級的人物（官員、戰士等）擁有更大的權力。

國王古地亞（Gudea）雕像刻有致城邦守護神的獻詞，可見米索不達米亞人的權力離不開宗教。
祭司的職責
祭司們按照其等級負責不同的宗教活動。高級祭司的義務包括主持祭祀儀式、透過占卜跟觀察天象預知未來和了解神意、以及安排較低級的祭司的工作。其他的祭司則有帶領人們唱聖歌、舉行驅魔儀式、替神廟建造雕像、打掃清潔等責任。

淨化儀式所用的水池。上面的魚形人物估計是身穿服裝的祭司。
除了跟宗教有關的工作外，祭司對國家的營運也有莫大的貢獻。身為塔廟的管理者，他們直接控制國家大量的財富。城邦有一部分的土地屬於廟產，祭司可以使用那兒的資源進行商業活動，繼而增長塔廟中的財產。另外，神廟内擺滿了國民給守護神的貢品，城邦打勝仗的戰利品也會優先送往塔廟。由此可見，掌控神廟的祭司理所當然是古代米索不達米亞社會的權勢者。值得一提的是，祭司還會在塔廟的外院裏幫人民治病。塔廟亦設有學校，由祭司充當其老師跟管理人。

用於祭祀的塔廟是每個城邦的中心建築。上圖的是吾珥城塔廟。

塔廟遺址發掘出的雕像，相信是獻給城邦守護神的祭品。
祭司的性別
祭司一職是世襲的，因此成為祭司的人通常屬於名門望族。即使古代社會重男輕女，女性也能夠成為祭司。根據《漢模拉比法典》，女祭司享有繼承父親遺產的權利。最有名的女祭司無疑是薩爾貢大帝（Sargon of Akkad）的女兒安海度亞娜（Enheduanna）。她曾以吾珥城高級祭司的身份創作了「蘇美神廟讚美詩」一系列的詩篇。

安海度亞娜的浮雕肖像，位於浮雕正中的她跟三名隨從主持祭祀儀式。

documents.filename: 2.5_Father of Renaissance Leonardo da Vinci_S.docx
documents.title: 2.5_Father of Renaissance Leonardo da Vinci_S
documents.content:
Father of Renaissance:
Leonardo da Vinci
About Leonardo da Vinci
Called as a ‘Renaissance man’ [文藝復興時代人物], Leonardo da Vinci was a gifted artist and a great inventor [發明家]. Born in 1492 in Italy, Leonardo da Vinci studied painting and sculpture in Florence. Sponsored [資助] by rich merchants, Leonardo da Vinci drew famous paintings. Afterwards, Leonardo da Vinci spent time on science, anatomy [解剖學] and art. Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo and Raphael were among the most famous Renaissance artists. In 1516, Leonardo da Vinci was invited by the King of France to promote art in France. In 1519, Leonardo da Vinci died at the age of 67.

This is the self-portrait of young Leonardo da Vinci.
Works of Leonardo da Vinci
Leonardo da Vinci was truly a ‘Renaissance man’ with great achievements in art. He adopted [採用] different painting skills, from simple pencil drawings [原筆素描] to oil paintings. Since he was interested in development of other aspects, he did not have many finished works. Despite this [儘管如此], all his works were very famous.

This is Leonardo da Vinci’s sketch called Female head (The Lady of the Dishevelled Hair).

This is Last Supper, one of Leonardo da Vinci’s famous works.

This is Mona Lisa painted by Leonardo da Vinci from 1503 to 1506.
Works of Leonardo da Vinci
Before the Renaissance, painters did not know special painting techniques to make the paintings look realistic and rich in detail. In the Renaissance, painters knew how to use different perspectives to present [表達] the facial expression of the characters. Based on his work, we could tell that Leonardo da Vinci used both linear perspective and atmospheric perspective.
Atmospheric Perspective
Leonardo da Vinci observed that when the farer away the objects are, the blurrier it becomes. Besides, the phenomenon of rain, objects and dusts also affected the object. The colours changed as the distance changed. Therefore, the landscape [風景] Leonardo da Vinci painted was often blurred. In the Virgin and Child with Saint Anne, the figures [人物] in the foreground [前景] was rich in colour and sharp in contour [輪廓]. In contrast, the landscape at the background was blurry and dull [暗淡].

Leonardo da Vinci’s painting Virgin and Child with Saint Anne.
Linear Perspective
Apart from atmospheric perspective, Leonardo da Vinci and other artists often used linear perspective to present [表達] depth [景深]. In this method, the artist often set a ‘vanishing point’ [消失點] in the painting. Then, the landscape of the painting will gradually become smaller at the end of this vanishing point. His work, Last Supper, also adopted this painting method.

An example of linear perspective painted by Leonardo da Vinci
Treasure of Italian Art
In conclusion, Leonardo da Vinci’s painting skills surpass [超越] the those in the Middle Ages. He adopted painting skills such as atmospheric perspective, linear perspective as well as light and shade. Artists across generations set him as a model. He left lasting impact on the world.

documents.filename: 2.5_Industrial Revolution and the Birth of Labour Law_S.docx
documents.title: 2.5_Industrial Revolution and the Birth of Labour Law_S
documents.content:
Industrial Revolution and the Birth of Labour Law
Although the Employment Ordinance has been passed for years, protection for working-class employees should be strengthened regarding to the fact that long working hours and uncompensated overtime are common. Nevertheless, do you know the history of employment laws?

‘Standard Working Hour’ legislation in Hong Kong has been a hot debate. When will the problem of long working hours be solved?
Labour Laws and Working Class Movements
The history of standing working hours and other labour legislation can be dated back to the Industrial Revolution between 18th and 19th centuries. A large number of workers were employed to work together in a factory after the factory system began. Therefore, labour relations had been important especially when there were different labour problems like low wages, industrial accidents, illnesses and child labour.
The British government passed Factory Acts in 1802, 1819 and 1825. However, the living and working conditions of the workers had not been improved much. In response to the factory movements in the 1930s, the government advocated reforms and passed new factory act. But the problems were still serious. Therefore, the workers formed unions and supported the Chartist Movement in Britain for demanding fewer working hours, higher wages and the right to vote. The working class movements forced the government to make amendments to the laws for satisfy both capitalists and the working class.

The Chartist Movement in Britain (1838-1848)
Child Labour
Child labour was very common. Though restrictions on working hours and age were imposed in the first three Factory Acts mentioned, such as children under 9 years old were prohibited from working in cotton mills and those less than 16 were restricted to a 12-hour day. Nevertheless, many employers did not comply with the rules and even said that they would bring about lower competiveness and higher crime rate.
The 1833 Factory Act had made a progress in protecting child labour. Children under 9 were not allowed to work. A maximum working week of 48 hours was set for those aged 9 to 13, limited to 8 hours a day; and for children between 13 and 18 it was limited to 12 hours daily. Working at nights were prohibited for the child labour. An inspection system was also established to ensure that regulations were enforced. After making number of amendments, the laws were firmly enforced eventually.

Child labour was a common topic after the factory system began..
First Industrial Employment Ordinance in Hong Kong
As a British colony, Hong Kong made its laws with reference to the British employment laws. Child labour was also a major problem in Hong Kong after Hong Kong was opened up. Children aged from 7 to 8 also worked as porters. Those worked in manufacturing industry had relatively longer working hours. Meanwhile, although they often had to do dangerous jobs, their wages were much lower than the adults. Most likely, they worked for increasing family income.
Thus, the first industrial employment ordinance in Hong Kong was Industrial Employment of Children Ordinance in 1922. The legal minimum age for working in a factory was set at 10 years but children under 15 were barred from working in dangerous industries. They worked 9 hours a day maximum and they could not work 5 hours consecutively. Every week they were entitled one day leave. Also, they were not allowed to work at nights. After that, Hong Kong made different regulations to protect the employees.

This is an ordinance to regulate the employment of children in certain industries (Ordinance No.22 of 1922).
Yet, labour issues have not been solved in Hong Kong due to the conflicts between employers and employees. However, the working class could only went on strikes and marches to express their views. There were very few organisations joining the movements just like Britain and the other Western countries. Therefore, it is important to gather the forces from the employees and unions in order to fight for a better protection.

documents.filename: 2.5_The Battle of Vienna Turning Point for the Ottoman Empire - S.docx
documents.title: 2.5_The Battle of Vienna Turning Point for the Ottoman Empire - S
documents.content:
The Battle of Vienna: Turning Point for the Ottoman Empire

In 1453, the Ottoman Turks took Constantinople and ended the Eastern Roman Empire.
After ending the Eastern Roman Empire, the Ottoman became stronger and entered a stage of expansion until they lost in the battle of Vienna in 1683. The battle also implied that the empire stopped its expansion to Europe. Christianity was able to defend the influences of Islam successfully. Apart from being an important war in history, do you know how coffee bean and croissant [牛角麵包] were related to the battle of Vienna?

Hagia Sophia was an ancient Byzantine cathedral, later an imperial mosque of the Ottoman Empire as a symbol of the Turks’ victory in 1453.

The Ottoman Empire in the 16th century, with a sphere of influence across much of Europe, Asia and Africa.
Development of the Battle of Vienna
In the early 17th century, the Ottman Empire entered in a period of decline. It hoped to restore its power through the invasion of Europe again. The Ottoman army advanced to Austria and prepared to occupy Vienna. Yet, the Austrian forces were not able to stop the attacks. The main Ottoman army finally laid siege to Vienna on 14 July. At that time, Austrian capital Vienna was defended by only 13,000, and they were not well-equipped with weapons and armaments. Although the Turkeys had already made a long way, they were still very strong. Therefore, they thought that it would be a simple task for them to take over the city. Because of this, they decided to take rest before the siege.

The Battle of Vienna
Due to the efforts made by the Viennaeses and their allies, the war turned into a stalemate. Nonetheless, the city was facing critical shortage of supplies. It seemed that the city was about to be captured. Then, the Polish king just on came and be the Chief Commander of the army. Finally, he successfully rescued Vienna. The victory of the Christian allies marked the historic end of the expansion of the Ottoman Empire into Europe.

Jan Sobieski, the king of Poland, was a great military leader who defeated the Turks at Khocizm in 1673 and Vienna in 1683.
Little Stories from the Battle
Although the Ottoman troops failed to capture Vienna, it was significant to the creation of Viennese coffee. 10 years before the siege, coffee bean had already been appeared in the Ottoman Empire.
After the war, the Viennese discovered many bags of coffee in the abandoned Ottoman encampment. The story goes on that, using this captured stock, Franciszek Jerzy Kulczycki opened the first coffeehouse in Vienna. He added milk and honey to sweeten the bitter coffee, and became the unique Viennese coffee. Until 1730, coffee had been imported from Christian countries.

The battle accidently led to a creation of Viennese coffee.
Besides, do you know why croissant shapes like the Crescent Moon? When Vienna was under siege, the Turks attempted to tunnel underneath the walls of the city. Some bakers hard at work in the middle of the night heard the sounds of the Turks digging and alerted the city’s defenders. The tunnel was blown up. The baker asked no reward other than the exclusive right to bake crescent-shaped pastries commemorating the incident.
However, it was not until 1770 that the pastry came to be known as the croissant when Austrian Princess married King Louis XVI of France.


The invention of croissant was also related to the battle of Vienna.
Conflict between Civilizations: Past and Continuity
The battle of Vienna signaled the end of the empire’s dominance. However, Christian-Muslim conflict never ends because of their differences in culture and religious belief. Given the turmoil in the Middle East and the rise of Islamic extremism, profound clashes between the two disparate civilizations are still prevalent in today’s world.

documents.filename: 2.5_The Bonfire of the Vanities Savonarola_s Discontent with the Renaissance_S.docx
documents.title: 2.5_The Bonfire of the Vanities Savonarola_s Discontent with the Renaissance_S
documents.content:
The Bonfire of the Vanities: Savonarola's Discontent with the Renaissance
In the Late Middle Ages, Europe suffered from famines (飢荒), plagues and wars. The Renaissance brought a glimmer of light to the dark times. It emphasised the value of human beings and opposed the Church’s control on people’s minds. The developments in this period left behind precious literature, art, architecture and scientific knowledge. However, there had been opposition towards these changes in Florence, the centre of the Italian Renaissance.
Florence and the Medici Family
Located in the central part of Italy, Florence gradually became one of the wealthiest Italian cities due to prosperous trade in the Late Middle Ages. The wealthy Medici family gained power in Florence, acting as the de facto ruler of Florence since 1434.

A map of Florence in the Nuremberg Chronicles published in 1493.
During the Renaissance, aristocrats [貴族] collected and commissioned [委約] art as an expression of knowledge, power and status. The Medici family keenly sponsored different kinds of art. Famous artists such as Michelangelo, Botticelli, Leonardo da Vinci and Raphael enjoyed their support. Under the rule of Lorenzo de’ Medici, a fifth-generation member of the Medici family, the Italian renaissance reached its peak. For this, he was called ‘Lorenzo the Magnificent’ by the people of his time.

Lorenzo de’ Medici and His Artists in the Sculpture Garden, work of Italian painter Ottavio Vannini
Opposition from Savonarola
In a prosperous city full of material wealth, people enjoyed secular lives [世俗生活] and spent a lot of money on art. The salvation of the spirit through Christ [耶穌基督的精神救贖] was neglected. This caused the dissatisfaction of people like Savonarola, a Catholic Dominican friar. At Lorenzo’s invitation, he came to Florence to preach about repentance [悔改] and reform. His way of preaching was quite radical: he claimed he could talk with God and the Saints directly. He also prophesised [預言] that Italy would be dragged into war and the Medici family would fall. He claimed that people should live humbly in order to be saved. His opposition of luxury won the support of the middle and lower classes.

Statue of Savonarola
Interestingly, some of Savonarola’s predictions gradually came true. In 1492, Lorenzo died and the Medici family became less powerful. In 1494, France invaded Florence and overthrew the Medici family. Savonarola took the chance to take power and established a new republic, becoming the new ruler of Florence.

A painting of Savonarola criticising luxury lives in Florence
Lighting a ‘Bonfire of the Vanities’
Savonarola disliked the developments of the Renaissance. After coming to power, he started to ban all activities that he viewed as immoral [不道德], such as drinking, gambling and playing chess. He wanted to bring back a simple religious lifestyle of worshipping God.
During the yearly Carnival [狂歡節] celebrations, a fire would be lit at the Piazza della Signoria [領主廣場]. This was known as the ‘bonfire of the vanities’ [虛榮之火]. One of the largest bonfires took place on 7 February 1497. Savonarola encouraged his supporters to collect different luxuries [世俗逸樂的物品] such as mirrors, cosmetics, fine clothing, jewellery, music instruments, gambling tools, as well as books, paintings and sculptures. All these objects that could ‘tempt people into sin’ [引人陷入罪惡] were burned by Savonarola’s supporters. Among the burnt items were famous literary works such as Boccaccio’s Decameron. Some sources claim that even the painter Botticelli was influenced by Savonarola and burned many of his paintings. Although these records are questionable, it is true that the bonfire of the vanities destroyed many works of the Renaissance.

Bonfires of the vanities were lit in the Piazza della Signoria.
Swallowed by the Fire
Savonarola’s influenced reached its peak with the bonfire of the vanities in 1497. However, his growing power also drew the attention of the Church. Meanwhile, some people in Florence thought Savonarola’s rule was too harsh. Moreover, Florence’s commerce declined; Savonarola’s promise to save Florence did not come true. All of this led to growing discontent. In May 1947, Pope Alexander VI excommunicated [開除教籍] Savonarola and asked Florence to arrest and execute him. In the following year, Savonarola was burnt for heresy [信仰異教] and sedition [發表煽動言論]. Ironically, the execution took place at the Piazza della Signoria where he lit the bonfire of the vanities.
